State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95874

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive Legal . . . Enforcement . . . Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5660             322 5901          322 0441          322 6444


May 1, 1979


William M. Owen
City Attorney
City of Davis
226 F Street
Davis, CA 95616

Dear Mr. Owen:

     This is in response to my conversation with you and
Councilmember Bill Kopper on April 6, 1979, and your letter of
confirmation which included a copy of relevant portions of the
Davis Housing Code.

     Based on the additional information you have provided,
this letter reverses the conclusion reached in our written
advice dated January 9, 1979. In that letter we reasoned as
follows:

     In this case, we have not been given specific
facts with respect to the anticipated effects of the
ordinance. However, barring specific facts to the
contrary, it would appear that the ordinance in
question would materially affect Mr. Copper's [sic]
source of income...since the ordinance undoubtedly
will significantly increase the demand for thermal
inspections in Davis. Accordingly, Mr. Copper [sic]
should disqualify himself from making, participating
in the making or using his official position to
influence the retrofit ordinance.

     At the time we gave you that advice, we knew that Mr. Kopper
was in the business of advising homeowners and other property
owners about the thermal efficiency of their buildings, and
that the proposed ordinance would establish minimal energy
conservation standards which all residences within the City of
Davis would have to meet at the time of sale. As a result of
our more recent communications, however, we have been informed

William M. Owen
May 1, 1979                                                   Page Two


that the retrofit ordinance will be an amendment to the City's
Housing Code and will add the thermal energy standards to several
other standards which houses must meet when they are sold.  The
Housing Code further provides for a mandatory inspection of
every house at the time of sale to determine whether all of
these standards are met.  These inspections must be carried out
by the building official of the City's community development
department ("the administrator" in the words of the Housing Code)
and the fee for these services is also established by ordinance.
Currently, the fee is set at approximately $20.  As a result of
the inspection, the administrator issues a certificate of oc-
cupancy (which includes a certification that no deficiencies
were noted) or a report of deficiencies.  The owner must obtain
such a certificate or report before entering into any contract
of sale.  The Housing Code also allows the owner to obtain such
a certificate at any time, whether or not a sale is contemplated,
upon payment of the prescribed fee.

Mr. Kopper could perform a similar inspection for a home-
owner and could advise them whether their home met the standards
of the Housing Code.  He could not, however, provide the necessary
certification or report, and he has said that his fee for such
an inspection would be approximately $40, significantly higher
than that charged by the City.  Given the fact that a homeowner
who wanted to sell his or her house and who had previously con-
sulted Mr. Kopper would nevertheless have to pay the City for a
duplicated inspection in order to obtain the required certifi-
cation or report, it does not seem that the adoption of the
retrofit ordinance will result in an increase in the demand for
private thermal inspections.

Finally, in our conversation Mr. Kopper indicated that
he does few of these inspections and that the scope of the
inspections he does do is different than that provided for in
the ordinance.  For example, the ordinance is addressed to
energy conservation in general and contains standards for con-
servation of water and electricity.  Mr. Kopper's advice is
related to the more narrow topic of thermal efficiency and
addresses that topic more closely, suggesting specific products
and ways to use those products to increase thermal efficiency.
It cannot be concluded, therefore, that the adoption of the
retrofit ordinance would cause a decrease in Mr. Kopper's business
either.

Accordingly, Mr. Kopper does not have to disqualify himself
from participating in the City Council's decision on the retrofit
ordinance as now proposed.  Of course, if there were significant
changes made in the proposal, for example to modify it to provide
for certification as a result of private inspections, such

William M. Owen
May 1, 1979                                                    Page Three


changes might create the possibility of a material financial
effect on Mr. Kopper's business and under such circumstances he
might be well advised to consult you or us to determine if his
participation would be prohibited by the Political Reform Act.

     If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate
to call us.

                                                 Sincerely,

                                                 [signature]
                                                 Sarah T. Cameron
                                                 Counsel
                                                 Conflicts of Interest Division

STC:mfa

CITY OF DAVIS

226 F Street
Davis, California 95616
(916) 756-3740

April 9, 1979

Ms. Sarah T. Cameron
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: City of Davis' Draft Energy
Retrofit Ordinance

Dear Ms. Cameron:

Thank you for taking time to discuss the above-
referenced matter with Councilman Bill Kopper and me.

As you requested, I am enclosing a copy of the
current City of Davis Code (relevant sections) relating to
the City's housing resale inspection ordinance. As you can
see, the resale inspection ordinance requires inspection by
the City Building Official of residential real property prior
to sale. Under section 6-22 the Building Official determines
compliance with the City's Housing Code, zoning ordinance
"and other ordinances of the City relating to health and
safety standards for residences."

The proposed energy retrofit ordinance would be
added to the list of ordinances with which the Building Official
determines compliance.

You will also note that section 6-27 allows an
owner or tenant to obtain a Building Official's compliance
inspection at any time by paying the appropriate fee.

In summary, as presently contemplated, the inspec-
tions for energy retrofit compliance would be accomplished
through the existing housing resale inspection procedure wherein
the owner pays an inspection fee to the City and the City's
Building Official conducts the inspection and enforcement. In
cases of residences which do not meet the energy performance
goals of the retrofit ordinance, it is our goal to have an
ordinance with specific identified energy conservation options
(ECTs) which the homeowner can install without necessity of
outside consultants.

Ms. Sarah T. Cameron
April 9, 1979
Page 2


          On behalf of Mr. Kopper, I request a reconsideration
of the conclusions reached in your letter of January 9, 1979,
in light of the above-recited facts. Specifically, it would
appear that the proposed ordinance will not ". . " increase
the demand for [private] thermal inspections in Davis."

          As I mentioned, the City Council will begin delibera-
tions on the proposed retrofit ordinance sometime in mid to
late May. Your efforts to determine Mr. Kopper's voting status
in this matter before that time will be appreciated.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]

                                        WILLIAM L. OWEN
                                        City Attorney

§ 6-19                          Davis City Code                              § 6-20

     (c)   Section 502(d), "Projections," is amended to read:

     "Projection may encroach into required yards as provided by Chapter 29
of the Code of the City of Davis, 1971 as amended."

     (d)   The following section is added to read as follows:

     "Section 1205.  Preliminary Appeal.

     "When mutually agreeable with the Building Official and appellant, a
preliminary hearing may be conducted in accordance with procedures set forth
in Section 204 of the Uniform Building Code, 1973 edition.

     "NOTE: The section references in subsections (a) through (d) refer
to section numbers of the Uniform Housing Code, 1973 edition."
(Ord. No. 820, § 3.)

     Division 2.  Certificates of Occupancy;
                  Reports of Deficiencies.

Sec. 8-19. Definitions.

     For the purposes of this division, the following words and phrases shall
have the meanings respectively ascribed to them by this section:

     Administrator. The building official of the community development de-
partment.

     City. The City of Davis.

     Person. An individual, partnership, corporation or association, or the
rental agent of any of the foregoing.

     Unit. A dwelling unit in a single-family, two-family or multi-family
residence building, motel, hotel, rooming or boarding house, fraternity,
sorority or similar living accommodation. (Ord. No. 820, § 4.)

Sec. 6-20. Required.

     No owner shall enter into an agreement to sell, exchange, rent or lease
with an option to sell or purchase, nor shall any person act as a salesman,
broker or agent in connection with the preparation of an agreement for the
sale, exchange, rental or lease with an option to sell or purchase any unit
until a certificate of occupancy or report of deficiencies has been issued by
the administrator as hereinafter provided. Prior to the sale, execution of a
contract of sale or of an agreement to rent or lease with an option to purchase
any unit within the city, the owner or salesman, broker or agent thereof shall
provide a copy of the certificate of occupancy or report of deficiencies to the


                                         50.12
                                                                    Supp. #14, 10-76

prospective purchaser of such unit.  The purchaser shall acknowledge receipt and the date of receipt of the certificate of occupancy or report of deficiencies on the original report or a true copy thereof.

     Nothing herein shall prevent the completion of an agreement for sale, lease with an option to purchase or deposit receipt containing an express statement as follows:

     "This agreement is subject to an inspection of the premises by the City of Davis prior to the completion of sale.  In the event that the inspection results in the issuance of a Report of Deficiencies, the buyer (offeror) may unilaterally, within five (5) days of the receipt of any such Report of Deficiencies, cancel this agreement."  (Ord. No. 820, § 5; Ord. No. 848, § 1.)

Sec. 6-21.  Application; fee.  When required by this division to obtain a certificate of occupancy or report of deficiencies, the owner shall file with the administrator or salesman, broker or agent thereof a written application for a certificate of occupancy or report of deficiencies on a form to be prescribed by the administrator, accompanied by the fee therefor.  (Ord. No. 820, § 6.)

Sec. 6-22.  Inspection.  Within two working days after the application is received and the owner requests an inspection or reinspection, the administrator shall cause an inspection of the unit to be made for compliance with the city's Housing Code, the Zoning Ordinance and other ordinances of the city relating to health and safety standards for residences.  If the unit is in compliance with such codes and ordinances, the administrator shall, within five working days of the inspection, issue a certificate of occupancy.  (Ord. No. 820, § 7; Ord. No. 848, § 1.)

Sec. 6-23.  Contents of certificate.  The certificate of occupancy shall state:

     (a)  The date of issue.

     (b)  The legal use and occupancy of the unit.

     (c)  The address of the building.

     (d)  The name of the person to whom the certificate is issued.

     (e)  The certification that as of the date of the inspection, no applicable code or ordinance deficiencies were noted in the unit.  (Ord. No. 820, § 8.)

50.13                                    (Davis 8/2/78)

§ 6-24                    Davis City Code                    § 6-28

Sec. 6-24. Contents of report. Where an inspection discloses
that a unit is not in compliance with such codes and ordinances,
the administrator shall, within five working days of the in-
spection, issue a report of deficiencies. Such report shall
itemize all deficiencies and shall contain the following state-
ment:

    "NOTICE TO PROSPECTIVE PURCHASER: You are hereby advised
that the residential unit described in this report has been
found to have certain deficiencies which are itemized herein.
You, as purchaser, will be required to correct listed defi-
ciencies which are not corrected prior to sale of the property."
(Orc. No. 820, § 7; Ord. No. 848, § 1.)

Sec. 6-25. Fees. The filing fee for the certificate of
occupancy or report of deficiencies shall be set forth in a
resolution of the city council establishing a schedule of fees
and charges for various classes of units subject to this
article. (Ord. No. 820, § 10; Ord. No. 949, § 2; Ord. No.
963.)

Division 3. Inspections; Appeals; Violations.

Sec. 6-26. Availability for inspection. The owner shall be
responsible for making the unit available for inspection by the
city. (Ord. No. 820, § 11.)

Sec. 6-27. Voluntary requests for inspection. Nothing con-
tained in this article shall prevent an owner or tenant from,
upon payment of the fee herein provided, voluntarily request-
ing inspection and certification at any time for the purpose
of determining whether the premises comply with law. (Ord.
No. 820, § 13.)

Sec. 6-28. Appeals. (a) Any person aggrieved by any deter-
mination of the administrator under this article, except as
provided for in subsection (b) of this section or as provided
in section 6-18(d) of this article, may appeal to the housing
advisory and appeals board by filing a written notice of appeal
with the secretary of the housing advisory and appeals board
within ten days of the determination being appealed.




(Davis 8/2/78)                50.14

§ 6-29                          Buildings                           § 6-30

     (b)  Any person aggrieved by any determination of the administrator
relating to a zoning ordinance violation, may appeal such determination by filing a written notice of appeal as required by this city Zoning Ordinance. (Ord.
No. 820, § 12.)

Sec. 6-29. Violations.

     (a)  Any person who violates any provision of this article shall be
deemed guilty of an infraction and, upon conviction thereof, shall be punished
by a fine not exceeding the maximum prescribed by law.

     (b)  As an additional remedy, a violation of this article which threatens
to be continuing in nature is hereby deemed and declared to be a public nuisance and may be abated summarily by a restraining order or injunction issued
by a court of competent jurisdiction. (Ord. No. 820, §§ 14, 15.)

                    Article IV.  Dead Bolt Locks.

Sec. 6-30. Definitions.

     For the purposes of this article, the following words and phrases shall
have the meanings respectively ascribed to them by this section:

     Cylinder guard.  A ring surrounding the exposed portion of the lock
cylinder or any other device which is so fastened as to protect the cylinder
from wrenching, prying, cutting or pulling by attack tools.

     Dead bolt.  A bolt which has no automatic spring action and which is
operated by a key cylinder, thumb-turn or lever, and is held fast when in the
projected position.

     Dwelling unit.  A single unit occupied for living purposes and providing
complete independent facilities for one or more persons, including permanent
provisions for living, sleeping, eating, cooking and sanitation.

     Exterior door.  Any hinged door in a wall which is exterior to the habitable area of a dwelling unit, including doors leading from garage areas to
habitable areas and doors leading from common areas, such as hallways,
to individual dwelling units.

     Group H occupancies.  Those structures used as hotels and apartment
houses.

     Group I occupancies.  Those structures used as dwellings and lodging
houses.

     Rental unit.  Any dwelling unit which is offered for rent, lease or hire,
or which is rented, leased or hired.


                                    50.15
                                                        Supp. #14, 10-76

§ 6-31                    Davis City Code                    § 8-33

Turnpiece. A manually operated metal device which, when turned in
one direction, causes the bolt to move into the projected position and which,
when turned in the opposite direction, retracts the bolt from the projected
position. (Ord. No. 837, § 2.)

Sec. 6-31. Scope--New construction.

(a)   The provisions of this article shall apply to every group II and
Group I occupancy structure for which a building permit is issued after the
effective date of this article.³

(b)   The provisions of this article shall also apply to any existing
Group II or Group I occupancy structure that undergoes additions, alterations
or repairs within any twelve-month period, when such additions, alterations
or repairs exceed twenty percent of the replacement value of the building.
(Ord. No. 837, § 3.)

Sec. 6-32. Same--Rental units; exceptions.

The owner or designated agent of the owner of all Group II and Group I
occupancies which contain rental units, shall place an order for immediate delivery of the lock hardware complying with section 6-33 of this article within
forty-five days of the adoption hereof.

All such Group II and Group I occupancies shall comply with the provisions of this article within sixty days of the delivery of the required lock hardware to the owner or designated agent of the owner.

The foregoing notwithstanding, dead bolt locks installed prior to the
effective date of this article and having a turnpiece with a minimum throw of
five-eighths of an inch shall not be required to be replaced or modified despite
nonconformity with section 6-33; unless such replacement or modification is required by future ordinance or other binding regulation. (Ord. No. 837, § 4;
Ord. No. 849, § 1.)

Sec. 6-33. Specifications and requirements.

(a)   Exterior doors shall have a single cylinder dead bolt lock, with
a turnpiece having a minimum throw of one inch.

(b)   Dead bolts shall contain a hardened core interior.

(c)   Dead bolts shall have a metal-to-metal interior construction, meaning any design by which components of the lock are held together so as to pre-

3.    Editor's note.--The ordinance from which this article
       is derived was adopted September 15, 1976 and
       became effective as provided by law.

                            50.16
                                                    Supp. #14, 10-76

§ 6-34                           Buildings                              § 6-38

vert the easy disememberment of the lock by the use of attacking tools. A mini-
mum of two metal bolts connecting the front to the rear of the lock is acceptable.

     (d)   So that they cannot be gripped by pliers or other wrenching de-
vices, dead bolts shall have a rotating and tapered cylinder guard.

     (e)   In order to prevent the forcing of the striker from the jamb, the
screws used to attach the striker plate to the door jamb shall be of sufficient
length to firmly anchor within the adjacent framing member. (Ord. No. 837,
§ 5.)

Sec. 6-34. Responsibility for compliance.

     The owner, or designated agent of the owner, shall be responsible for
compliance with the specifications set forth in this article. (Ord. No. 837,
§ 6.)

Sec. 6-35. Enforcement.

     The provisions of this article shall be enforced by the building official.
(Ord. No. 837, § 7.)

Sec. 6-36. Appeals.

     In order to prevent or lessen unnecessary hardship or practical difficul-
ties in exceptional cases where it is difficult or impossible to comply with the
strict letter of this article, the owner, or designated agent of the owner, may
file a written appeal with the board of building appeals. The board of building
appeals may allow or deny such appeal; provided, that no appeal shall be
granted unless substantially equivalent security devices are provided. (Ord.
No. 837, § 8.)

Sec. 6-37. Alternatives.

     The enforcing authority or board of building appeals may approve any
alternative to the material or method of construction prescribed in this article;
provided, that the enforcing authority finds that the material and proposed de-
sign is satisfactory and at least equivalent to that prescribed in this article.
(Ord. No. 837, § 9.)

Sec. 6-38. Violations.

     Any person, firm or corporation violating any of the provisions of this
article shall be deemed guilty of an infraction and, upon conviction thereof,
shall be punished by a fine not exceeding the maximum prescribed by law.

     Each day that a violation of this article shall continue shall constitute a
separate offense. (Ord. No. 837, § 10.)


                                    50.17                          Supp. #14, 10-76

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 907 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95811

Technical Assistance • • • Advice on ... • ... Executive legal .... Enforcement .... State of Informa...
916 322 5660          322 5660                 322 5691          322 5648


January 9, 1979


A-79-122

William L. Owen
City Attorney
City of Davis
226 F Street
Davis, California 95616

Dear Mr. Owen:

     Thank you for your letter of December 15, 1978, requesting
advice with respect to the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act.  I hope the following informal advice
pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and Commission
Regulation 18329 is helpful.

     The facts as I understand them are as follows:  William
Copper, a Davis City Councilman, as part of his private consulting business, advises homeowners and other property owners about
the thermal efficiency of buildings.  Generally, a calculation
of thermal efficiency is made by inspecting the building for
such things as insulation, window shading, building orientation
and similar factors.  Mr. Copper's reports include recommendations
for improving the buildings' thermal efficiency and
specifics the names of brand products which might be used and
contractors who could perform the suggested modifications.
Mr. Copper does not receive compensation from manufacturers or
contractors recommended in his reports.  He does receive a fee
from the client for whom he performs the consulting services.

     As the result of a study performed with funds provided by
the California State Energy Commission, an ordinance has been
proposed which would require residences sold within the city of
Davis to be brought up to certain minimal energy conservation
standards.  This ordinance would apply to buildings constructed
prior to the City's 1975 adoption of energy conservation standards
for new structures.

William L. Owen
Page Two                                January 9, 1979


Government Code Section 87100 provides:

     No public official at any level of state or
     local government shall make, participate in making
     or in any way attempt to use his official position
     to influence a governmental decision in which he
     knows or has reason to know he has a financial
     interest.

As you stated in your letter, Mr. Copper's business is a source
of income to him pursuant to Section 87103(c).1/

Commission Regulation 18702 (enclosed) provides that the
foreseeable affects of a governmental decision on a source of
income will be material if the amount of the official's income
will be affected; if there is a nexus between the governmental
decision and the purpose for which the official receives income;
or if the assets or revenues of the business entity will be
affected by certain threshold amounts. In this case, we have
not been given specific facts with respect to the anticipated
effects of the ordinance. However, barring specific facts to
the contrary, it would appear that the ordinance is questionable
as it materially affect Mr. Copper's source of income under all
three standards since the ordinance undoubtedly will significantly
increase the demand for thermal inspections in Davis. Accordingly,
Mr. Copper should disqualify himself from making, participating in
the making or using his official position to influence the retrofit
ordinance.

If I may be of further assistance, please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Ted Prim, Chief
                                        Conflict of Interest Division

                                        [illegible signature]

By:  Sarah T. Cameron
     Counsel
     Conflict of Interest Division

TP:SC:mc

Enclosure


1/ The business probably constitutes an investment to Mr. Copper as
     well as pursuant to Sections 87103(a) and 87014.

CITY OF DAVIS

226 F Street    Davis, California 95616
(916) 756-3740

December 15, 1978


Mr. Michael Bennett
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re:  Request for Written Advice
     Conflict of Interest

Dear Mr. Bennett:

     Pursuant to Title 2 California Administrative Code
Section 18329, I am hereby requesting written advice on behalf
of William Kopper, Davis City Councilman.

     Councilman Kopper is a law student at the Davis
Campus of the University of California and, in addition, he
operates a private consulting service from which he has
derived more than $250 in income during the preceding 12-month
period. The consulting service provides advice to homeowners
and other property owners regarding the thermal efficiency of
buildings.

     Mr. Kopper's standard practice is to conduct an on-
site survey of the client's building. The survey includes an
evaluation of existing insulation, window shading devices,
building orientation and other pertinent measurements. A
calculation of thermal efficiency is made, and Mr. Kopper then
prepares a written report to the property owner containing the
calculation of existing thermal performance of the subject
structure, as well as recommendations for modifications which
can improve the performance of the buildings. Mr. Kopper's
reports include recommendations of specific brand products and
contractors who can perform the recommended modifications.
Mr. Kopper does not receive compensation from the manufacturers
or contractors who are recommended in his report. Mr. Kopper's
only compensation is a fee paid by the client for the consulting
service.

Mr. Michael Bennett
December 15, 1978
Page 2


The State of California Energy Commission provided
funds to the City of Davis to perform a study of the feasi-
bility of enacting an energy conservation retrofit ordinance.
The City contracted with the firm of Sedway/Cooke for
purposes of completing the retrofit ordinance study. As a
result of that study, Sedway/Cooke has proposed an energy
conservation retrofit ordinance which would require residences
sold within the City of Davis to be brought to certain minimal
energy conservation standards. This ordinance would apply to
the sale of existing buildings which were constructed prior to
the City's 1975 adoption of energy conservation standards for
new structures. A copy of the proposed ordinance is enclosed
for your information.

Councilman Kopper does not believe that his consulting
work poses any conflict of interest with voting upon the
proposed retrofit ordinance.

The specific question is whether Mr. Kopper is
precluded from considering and acting as a City Councilman with
reference to the proposed energy conservation retrofit ordinance.

Very truly yours,

WILLIAM L. OWEN
City Attorney

enclosure

cc: Dwight Dickerson, Staff Attorney

DAVIS ENERGY CONSERVATION
RETROFIT PROGRAM

PROPOSED ENERGY CONSERVATION
RETROFIT ORDINANCE




Prepared for the

CITY OF DAVIS




By

SEDWAY/COOKE
Urban and Environmental
Planners and Designers




July 7, 1978

ORDINANCE NO. _______


An ordinance amending the Housing Code for the City of Davis to establish energy
conservation standards for existing residential structures within the City of Davis.

The City Council of the City of Davis does hereby ordain as follows:


SECTION 1. FINDINGS

A. The electrical and natural gas energy used to power the climate control and
habitability systems of residential structures is essential to the health, safety,
and welfare of the people of the State of California. The cost of energy is rapidly
rising due to uncertainties about present and future supplies of natural gas, and
the inability of powerplant construction to keep pace with the rising demand for
electricity. Rising residential energy costs are becoming an increasing economic
burden for lower and middle-income families and persons.

B. Almost all existing residential structures in the State of California were
constructed during a period of relative energy abundance. Therefore, most of
these structures employ climate control and habitability systems which consume
energy at levels exceeding those which are possible if recently developed and
previously existing energy conservation technologies are employed.

Significant opportunities exist for energy conservation through the application
of appropriate technologies to existing residential structures. Conservation of
energy in this manner would result in decreased residential energy bills and would
diminish the threat to the health and welfare of residents of this state which is
posed by potential future energy shortages.

C. The Housing Code of the City of Davis presently does not address energy
conservation in existing residential structures. The climate control and habit-
ability systems employed in many of these structures consequently consume energy at

rates which exceed those which could be obtained if the Housing Code did provide
standards requiring the installation of appropriate energy conservation technologies.
These excessive rates of energy consumption, in the face of rising energy prices,
adversely affect the present and future ability of the occupants of these residences
to purchase the energy necessary to adequately maintain the climate control and
habitability systems essential to their health and welfare. Amendment of the
Housing Code to establish energy conservation standards as part of the housing
resale inspection program is therefore necessary to protect the health and welfare
of the occupants of housing within the City of Davis.


SECTION 2. PURPOSE

The purpose and intent of this ordinance is to prescribe, by regulation, standards
which increase efficiency in the use of energy by residential structures. These
standards accomplish this goal by requiring the application of appropriate energy
conservation technologies to the climate control and habitability systems of these
structures.


SECTION 3. DEFINITIONS

ECT: An energy conservation technology, as specified in this ordinance.

Unit: A dwelling unit in a single-family, two-family, or multi-family residence
building, motels, hotels, rooming and boarding houses, fraternities, sororities and
similar living accommodations. (This definition is contained in the Davis Housing
Code and is repeated here only for clarity.)

Unit Type:

A: A dwelling unit with a slab-on-grade floor, a flat roof, and plywood siding

B: A dwelling unit with a slab-on-grace floor, a flat roof, and stucco siding;

ILLUSTRATION I*

TO DWELLING UNIT TYPE

                                                      tion Opportunity

             Flat roof (flat)                         Low
             Plywood siding (plywood)                 Low

             Stucco siding (stucco)                   Medium
             Flat                                     Low

             Plywood                                  Low
             Flat                                     Low

             Stucco                                   Medium
             Flat                                     Low

             Plywood                                  Low
             Roof with attic (attic)                  High

             Stucco                                   Medium
             Roof with attic (attic)                  High

G            Raised                                   Medium
             Plywood                                  Low
             Attic                                    High

H            Raised                                   Medium
             Stucco                                   Medium
             Attic                                    High


*Not a part of ordinance

C: A dwelling unit with a floor raised above grade over an accessible crawl space,
a flat roof, and plywood sidings

D: A dwelling unit with a floor raised above grade over an accessible crawl space,
a flat roof, and stucco sidings

E: A dwelling unit with a slab-on-grade floor, an accessible attic space, and plywood
sidings

F: A dwelling unit with a slab-on-grade floor, an accessible attic space, and stucco
sidings

G: A dwelling unit with a floor raised above grade over an accessible crawl space,
an accessible attic space, and plywood siding;

H: A dwelling unit with a floor raised above grade over an accessible crawl space,
an accessible attic space, and stucco siding.

Accessible Crawl Space: A space between the floor and finished grade below
in a residential building having a vertical clearance of at least thirty (30) inches
from the top of the finished grade to the underside of the subfloor. A raised-above-
grade floor which is not atop an accessible crawl space shall be considered to
be a slab-on-grade floor.

Accessible Attic Space: A space between the roof and ceiling next below in a
residential building, such that the roof slope is not less than two and one-half
(2½) feet in twelve feet and the vertical clear height from the top of the bottom
chord of the truss or ceiling joist to the underside of the roof sheathing at the
roof ridge is at least thirty (30) inches.

Single-Family Residence Building Equivalence Factor: That number which is cal-
culated by multiplying six (6) by the total number of units located in a multi-
unit residential building which contains a particular unit.

sections.conclusion: Accordingly, Mr. Kopper does not have to disqualify himself from participating in the City Council's decision on the retrofit ordinance as now proposed. Of course, if there were significant changes made in the proposal, for example to modify it to provide for certification as a result of private inspections, such changes might create the possibility of a material financial effect on Mr. Kopper's business and under such circumstances he might be well advised to consult you or us to determine if his participation would be prohibited by the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Councilmember Bill Kopper must disqualify himself from voting on a proposed energy retrofit ordinance that would establish thermal energy conservation standards for residential properties, given his business providing thermal efficiency consulting services to homeowners.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC reversed its prior January 9, 1979 advice and concluded that Councilmember Kopper does not need to disqualify himself from the retrofit ordinance vote because the ordinance will not materially affect his business. The mandatory city inspections with certification authority, combined with Kopper's limited scope of work and higher fees, mean the ordinance will neither increase nor decrease demand for his private thermal inspection services.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the May 1, 1979 FPPC response letter and the April 9, 1979 City Attorney request letter. The FPPC response explicitly reverses its January 9, 1979 written advice. Housing Code excerpts are included as supporting documentation. Minor typo in original ('Copper' instead of 'Kopper') noted in quoted passages.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Councilmember Bill Kopper must disqualify himself from voting on a proposed energy retrofit ordinance that would establish thermal energy conservation standards for residential properties, given his business providing thermal efficiency consulting services to homeowners.

CONCLUSION: Accordingly, Mr. Kopper does not have to disqualify himself from participating in the City Council's decision on the retrofit ordinance as now proposed. Of course, if there were significant changes made in the proposal, for example to modify it to provide for certification as a result of private inspections, such changes might create the possibility of a material financial effect on Mr. Kopper's business and under such circumstances he might be well advised to consult you or us to determine if his participation would be prohibited by the Political Reform Act.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95874 Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive Legal . . . Enforcement . . . Conflict of Interest (916) 322 5660 322 5901 322 0441 322 6444 May 1, 1979 William M. Owen City Attorney City of Davis 226 F Street Davis, CA 95616 Dear Mr. Owen: This is in response to my conversation with you and Councilmember Bill Kopper on April 6, 1979, and your letter of confirmation which included a copy of relevant portions of the Davis Housing Code. Based on the additional information you have provided, this letter reverses the conclusion reached in our written advice dated January 9, 1979. In that letter we reasoned as follows: In this case, we have not been given specific facts with respect to the anticipated effects of the ordinance. However, barring specific facts to the contrary, it would appear that the ordinance in question would materially affect Mr. Copper's [sic] source of income...since the ordinance undoubtedly will significantly increase the demand for thermal inspections in Davis. Accordingly, Mr. Copper [sic] should disqualify himself from making, participating in the making or using his official position to influence the retrofit ordinance. At the time we gave you that advice, we knew that Mr. Kopper was in the business of advising homeowners and other property owners about the thermal efficiency of their buildings, and that the proposed ordinance would establish minimal energy conservation standards which all residences within the City of Davis would have to meet at the time of sale. As a result of our more recent communications, however, we have been informed William M. Owen May 1, 1979 Page Two that the retrofit ordinance will be an amendment to the City's Housing Code and will add the thermal energy standards to several other standards which houses must meet when they are sold. The Housing Code further provides for a mandatory inspection of every house at the time of sale to determine whether all of these standards are met. These inspections must be carried out by the building official of the City's community development department ("the administrator" in the words of the Housing Code) and the fee for these services is also established by ordinance. Currently, the fee is set at approximately $20. As a result of the inspection, the administrator issues a certificate of oc- cupancy (which includes a certification that no deficiencies were noted) or a report of deficiencies. The owner must obtain such a certificate or report before entering into any contract of sale. The Housing Code also allows the owner to obtain such a certificate at any time, whether or not a sale is contemplated, upon payment of the prescribed fee. Mr. Kopper could perform a similar inspection for a home- owner and could advise them whether their home met the standards of the Housing Code. He could not, however, provide the necessary certification or report, and
embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter reverses prior guidance regarding Councilmember Bill Kopper's conflict of interest regarding a Davis housing retrofit ordinance. Based on additional factual clarification about how the ordinance operates through mandatory city inspections rather than private certifications, the FPPC determined Kopper has no material financial interest requiring disqualification.
